planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm (70°F+); in zone 9 plant March–May. Sugar Baby produces 7–10 lb icebox-sized fruits — small enough to fit in a refrigerator whole. Judge ripeness by the tendril nearest the fruit turning brown and dry, the ground spot (where it rests on soil) turning yellow, and a dull hollow sound when thumped. In zone 9 watermelons perform excellently in summer heat; provide consistent moisture during fruit development but reduce watering as fruits approach maturity to concentrate sweetness. Allow 6–8 feet between hills for the sprawling vines.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral sandy or loamy soil — watermelons require deep, loose, well-drained soil and abundant heat to develop properly; Sugar Baby is the classic icebox watermelon variety — the small, round, dark-green fruits (7–10 pounds) that fit neatly into a home refrigerator, in contrast to the large oblong types (20–30+ pounds) traditional in Southern watermelon culture. The small fruit size makes Sugar Baby the most practical watermelon for small family gardens where a single large watermelon represents more than could be consumed before spoiling. The bright red, crisp, very sweet flesh with few seeds has made Sugar Baby one of the most popular home garden watermelons for decades — reliable, productive, and delivering the classic watermelon experience in a manageable size. As a true tropical fruit, watermelon is perfectly suited to zone 9's long, hot summers — in fact, the consistent 90°F+ temperatures of June through August produce the sweetest, most flavorful watermelons possible, as the combination of intense sun and heat maximizes sugar development in the fruit.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Sugar Baby Watermelon Description
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Watermelon Production for Gulf South
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Watermelon Growing Guide

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm (70°F+); in zone 9 plant March–May. Ambrosia is an open-pollinated variety requiring the full 84–90 days; succession is not practical but a single well-timed planting in zone 9 produces abundantly through summer. Ripe Ambrosia melons slip easily from the vine with a gentle tug — full-slip is the traditional ripeness indicator for cantaloupe. Reduce watering in the final 2 weeks before harvest to intensify sweetness and flavor. The powdery mildew resistance makes Ambrosia particularly reliable in zone 9's humid summer conditions.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil — melons require warm soil, abundant sun, and good air circulation; Ambrosia is an All-America Selections award-winning cantaloupe (muskmelon) that earned recognition for its exceptionally sweet, deeply aromatic, thick-fleshed, orange interior — with sugar content and fragrance significantly exceeding most commercial cantaloupe varieties bred for shipping durability. The medium-sized, rounded fruits with heavily netted tan rind and the unmistakable, intensely musky-sweet fragrance that announces ripeness from several feet away represent the classic cantaloupe eating experience at its best. Most commercial cantaloupes are harvested before full-slip (when they still cling to the vine) to withstand shipping without bruising — the result is fruit that is often underripe, bland, and lacking the musky sweetness that a vine-ripened cantaloupe develops. A homegrown Ambrosia harvested at true full-slip ripeness is fundamentally different from any commercial cantaloupe — genuinely aromatic, extremely sweet, and with a perfumed complexity that makes tasting it for the first time a revelation about what cantaloupe actually tastes like. Powdery mildew resistance makes Ambrosia reliable in the humid conditions of zone 9 summer.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cantaloupe Production for Gulf South
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Muskmelon/Cantaloupe Growing Guide

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Appalachian sedge is slow to establish but very long-lived once settled — allow 1–2 growing seasons for full establishment and spreading. It naturalizes readily from seed in suitable conditions, forming expanding clumps that can eventually replace turf grass in shaded areas. In zone 9 it performs as a reliable, low-growing, semi-evergreen to evergreen ground cover. Unlike many sedges that require moist soil, Appalachian sedge tolerates dry shade under mature trees — one of its most valuable practical attributes.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained to dry, moderately fertile to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Appalachian sedge is native to dry woodland habitats and tolerates the difficult conditions of dry shade under mature trees; Appalachian sedge is a fine-textured, low-growing native sedge endemic to the Appalachian region and extending through the Gulf South, providing an ecologically appropriate, low-maintenance alternative to lawn grass or imported ground covers in shaded landscapes. The narrow, arching, bright green to yellow-green leaves form a low, graceful, grass-like mound 6–10 inches high that spreads slowly by rhizomes and self-seeding to form a naturalistic, weed-suppressing ground cover under trees. As a native species adapted to the woodland understory of the Southeastern United States, Appalachian sedge supports native invertebrates and serves as a host plant for several native skipper butterflies, contributing ecological function along with aesthetic value. Unlike turf grass, Appalachian sedge requires no mowing, very little fertilization, and tolerates the dry shade and root competition under mature trees that eliminates conventional lawn alternatives — its primary competitive advantage as a practical garden plant. The low-growing, flowing texture provides a natural woodland floor aesthetic compatible with native shade tree and shrub plantings.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Carex appalachicola Native Plant
North Carolina State University Extension — Native Sedges for Shade Ground Cover
University of Georgia Extension — Native Plants for Southern Shade Gardens

planting.note: In zone 9 start seeds indoors August–September and transplant October–November for winter and early spring bloom. Stock requires vernalization (6–8 weeks of temperatures below 50°F at night) to initiate flowering in most varieties — zone 9 winters typically provide enough cool nights for flowering from December through March. The Vintage series was selected for lower vernalization requirements, making it better adapted to mild-winter climates than older stock varieties. Grow as a winter annual in zone 9; plants rarely survive into summer heat.

directions: Plant in full sun to light partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil — stock performs poorly in acidic soil and benefits from lime in naturally acidic garden soils; Vintage White stock (Matthiola incana) is one of the most intensely fragrant cut flowers available to home gardeners — the vertical spikes of pure white, densely petaled, ruffled flowers produce a rich, warm, vanilla-and-clove fragrance that is among the strongest and most complex of any garden flower. The fragrance of stock is categorically different from most flowers: warm, spicy, and complex rather than floral-sweet, filling an entire room from a single cut stem. Stock flowers are primarily valued as cut flowers for winter and early spring arrangements where the long, sturdy stems (18–24 inches) and exceptional fragrance make them among the most sought-after seasonal flowers for bouquets and arrangements. The Vintage series was developed for earliness and adaptation to warmer winter climates, making it practical for zone 9 winter gardens where adequate cold for vernalization is reliable but extreme cold is uncommon. Vintage White provides a classic, sophisticated white cut flower from December through March in zone 9 — a season when relatively few flowers are available.
sources: PanAmerican Seed — Matthiola Vintage Series Introduction
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cool-Season Flowers for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Winter Annual Cut Flowers for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Red Thread is a very low-growing (2–4 inch) alternanthera variety — one of the smallest in the genus — making it ideal for edging, carpet bedding patterns, and fine-scale foreground planting. In zone 9 it thrives in summer heat and performs continuously until frost. Like all alternanthera, it is grown purely as a foliage plant — the tiny, white flowers that may appear are insignificant and the plant should be sheared back if flowering begins to redirect energy into colorful new foliage.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Red Thread is a low-growing, very fine-textured alternanthera with narrow, thread-like, red-orange to coppery-red leaves — the extremely small leaf size and the thread-like character of the foliage create a fine, delicate texture quite different from the broader-leaved alternanthera varieties. The fiery orange-red to copper-red color of the threadlike leaves provides intense, saturated color in formal edging and carpet bedding where precision and consistent low growth are required. Red Thread is the alternanthera most commonly used in Victorian-inspired carpet bedding designs where differently colored low-growing foliage plants are arranged in precise geometric patterns — the tiny leaf size allows precise edging lines of 2–4 inches high that maintain crisp formal patterns throughout the growing season. In zone 9's summer heat, alternanthera's heat tolerance makes it one of the few ornamental foliage plants that can be used for formal, labor-intensive carpet bedding patterns without the summer decline and replanting that makes formal summer bedding impractical in cool climates.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alternanthera for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Summer Bedding Plants for Gulf Coast
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Foliage Annuals
